Buddha's teachings tended to promote gender equality as the Buddha held that women had the same spiritual capacities as men did. According to {{ill|Isaline Blew Horner|en|Isaline Blew Horner|qid=Q3155117|lt=Isaline Blew Horner|1=Isaline Blew Horner}}, women in Buddhist India: "commanded more respect and Ranked as individuals. They enjoyed more independence, and a wider liberty to guide and follow their own lives."<ref>IB Horner, Women Under Primitive Buddhism, 1930, 82.</ref> Buddha gave the same teachings to both sexes, praised various female lay disciples for their wisdom and allowed women to become monastics ([[:en:Bhikkhuni|Bhikkhuni]]s) at a time when this was seen as scandalous in India, where men dominated the spiritual professions. The two chief female disciples of the Buddha were {{ill|Khema|en|Khema|qid=Q2627201|lt=Khema|1=Khema}} and {{ill|Uppalavanna|en|Uppalavanna|qid=Q3175198|lt=Uppalavanna|1=Uppalavanna}}. The Buddha taught that women had the same [[:en:Soteriological|Soteriological]] potential as men, and that gender had no influence on one's ability to advance spiritually to nirvana. In the early Buddhist texts, female enlightened [[:en:Arhats|Arhats]] are common. Buddhist nuns are however bound by an extra 8 precepts not applicable to Buddhist monks called [[:en:The Eight Garudhammas|The Eight Garudhammas]]. The authenticity of these rules is highly contested; they were supposedly added to the ([[:en:Bhikkhuni|Bhikkhuni]]s) {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|qid=Q848764|lt=Vinaya|1=Vinaya}} "to allow more acceptance" of a monastic Order for women, during the Buddha's time but can be interpreted as a form of {{ill|gender discrimination|en|gender discrimination|qid=Q106230553|lt=gender discrimination|1=Gender discrimination}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.dhammadharini.net/dhamma-talks-from-the-bhikkhuni-sangha/aranya-bodhi-hermitage/non-historicity-of-the-eight-garudhammas|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103030748/http://www.dhammadharini.net/dhamma-talks-from-the-bhikkhuni-sangha/aranya-bodhi-hermitage/non-historicity-of-the-eight-garudhammas|url-status=dead|title=On the Apparent Non-historicity of the Eight Garudhammas Story|archivedate=November 3, 2013|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.bhikkhuni.net/library/anaalayo-gender-discrimination-canon.html|title=Alliance for Bhikkhunis - Gender Discrimination in the Pali Canon|date=July 26, 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100726221345/http://www.bhikkhuni.net/library/anaalayo-gender-discrimination-canon.html |archive-date=2010-07-26 |qq=}}</ref> Alan Sponberg argues that the early Buddhist sangha sought social acceptance through 'institutional {{ill|androcentrism|en|androcentrism|qid=Q514337|lt=androcentrism|1=Androcentrism}}' as it was dependent on material support from lay society. Because of this Sponberg concludes: "For all its commitment to inclusiveness at the doctrinal level, institutional Buddhism was not able to (or saw no reason to) challenge prevailing attitudes about gender roles in society."<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nku.edu/~gartigw/teaching_files/Sponberg,%20Alan%20%20(1992)%20-%20Attitudes%20toward%20Women%20and%20the%20Feminine%20in%20Early%20Buddhism.pdf|title=Sponberg, Attitudes toward Women and the Feminine in Early Buddhism, 1992|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160804174747/http://www.nku.edu/~gartigw/teaching_files/Sponberg,%20Alan%20%20(1992)%20-%20Attitudes%20toward%20Women%20and%20the%20Feminine%20in%20Early%20Buddhism.pdf |archive-date=2016-08-04 |qq=}}</ref> The pre-Mahayana texts also state that while women can become Arhats, they cannot become a [[Buddhahood#Samyaksambuddha|Samyaksambuddha]] (a Buddha who discovers the path by himself), [[:en:Chakravartin|Chakravartin]]s (Wheel turning king), a {{ill|Śakra (Buddhism)|en|Śakra (Buddhism)|qid=Q1444745|lt=Ruler of heaven|1=Śakra (Buddhism)}}, a {{ill|Mara (demon)|en|Mara (demon)|qid=Q1352021|lt=Mara|1=Mara (demon)}} devil or a {{ill|Brahmā (Buddhism)|en|Brahmā (Buddhism)|qid=Q289162|lt=Brahama god|1=Brahmā (Buddhism)}}.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 371-72.</ref> The [[:en:Therigatha|Therigatha]] is a collection of poems from elder Buddhist nuns, and one of the earliest texts of women's literature. Another important text is the Therī-{{ill|Apadāna|en|Apadāna|qid=Q4778977|lt=Apadāna|1=Apadāna}}, which collects the biographies of eminent nuns. One such verses are those of the nun Soma, who was tempted by {{ill|Mara (demon)|en|Mara (demon)|qid=Q1352021|lt=Mara|1=Mara (demon)}} when traveling in the woods. Mara states that women are not intelligent enough to attain enlightenment, Soma replies with a verse which indicates the insignificance of gender to spirituality: :"What does womanhood matter at all :When the mind is concentrated well, :When knowledge flows on steadily :As one sees correctly into Dhamma. :One to whom it might occur, :'I'm a woman' or 'I'm a man' :Or 'I'm anything at all' — :Is fit for Mara to address."<ref>Bhikkhu Bodhi, Discourses of the Ancient Nuns (Bhikkhuni-samyutta) © 1997, {{cite web|url=http://www.accesstoinsight.org/lib/authors/bodhi/bl143.html |title=Discourses of the Ancient Nuns: (Bhikkhuni-samyutta) |access-date=2008-12-27 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160420152439/http://www.accesstoinsight.org/lib/authors/bodhi/bl143.html |archive-date=2016-04-20 |qq=}}</ref> [[File:Haishangguanyin.jpg|thumb|The [[:en:Guan Yin of the South Sea of Sanya|Guan Yin of the South Sea of Sanya]] is the largest statue of a woman in the world.]] In {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}}, Bodhisattvas such as {{ill|Tara (Buddhism)|en|Tara (Buddhism)|qid=Q746709|lt=Tara|1=Tara (Buddhism)}} and [[Kannon|Guanyin]] are very popular female deities. Some {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Buddhist Tantric|1=Vajrayana}} texts include female consorts for each heavenly Buddha or Bodhisattva. In these Tantric couples, the female symbolizes wisdom ({{ill|Prajñā (Buddhism)|en|Prajñā (Buddhism)|qid=Q849649|lt=prajna|1=Prajñā (Buddhism)}}) and the male symbolizes skillful means ({{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|qid=Q581473|lt=Upaya|1=Upaya}}).<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 363.</ref> The union of these two qualities is often depicted as sexual union, known as {{ill|Yab-Yum|en|Yab-Yum|qid=Q2140227|lt=Yab-Yum|1=Yab-Yum}} (father-mother). In {{ill|East Asia|en|East Asia|qid=Q27231|lt=East Asia|1=East Asia}}, the idea of [[:en:Buddha nature|Buddha nature]] being inherent in all beings is taken to mean that, spiritually at least, the sexes are equal, and this is expressed by the {{ill|Śrīmālādevī Siṃhanāda Sūtra|en|Śrīmālādevī Siṃhanāda Sūtra|qid=Q1046397|lt=Lion's Roar of Queen Srimala sutra|1=Śrīmālādevī Siṃhanāda Sūtra}}. Based on this ideal of Buddha nature, the Chinese Chan ({{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}) school emphasized the equality of the sexes. {{ill|Dahui Zonggao|en|Dahui Zonggao|qid=Q1008018|lt=Dahui Zonggao|1=Dahui Zonggao}} (1089–1163) of the Chinese {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Linji school|1=Rinzai school}} said of women in Buddhism: "For mastering the truth, it does not matter whether one is male or female, noble or base."<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 366.</ref> The Japanese founder of [[:en:Soto Zen|Soto Zen]], [[:en:Dogen|Dogen]] wrote: "If you wish to hear the Dharma and put an end to pain and turmoil, forget about such things as male and female. As long as delusions have not yet been eliminated, neither men nor women have eliminated them; when they are all eliminated and true reality is experienced, there is no distinction of male and female."<ref>Francis Harold Cook, Francis Dojun Cook, Eihei Dogen; How to Raise an Ox: Zen Practice as Taught in Master Dogen's Shobogenzo, page 105.</ref> The attitude of Buddhists towards gender has been varied throughout history as it has been influenced by each particular culture and belief system such as {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} (which sees women as subservient) and {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hinduism|1=Hinduism}}. The Theravadin commentator {{ill|Buddhaghosa|en|Buddhaghosa|qid=Q335247|lt=Buddhaghosa|1=Buddhaghosa}} (5th century CE) for example, seems to have been influenced by his [[Brahmin]] background in stating that rebirth as a male is higher than rebirth as a female.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 370.</ref> Some Mahayana sutras such as the 'Sutra on Changing the Female Sex' and the 'Questions of the Daughter Pure Faith' also echo this idea. For various historical and cultural reasons such as wars and invasions, the orders of ordained Buddhist nuns disappeared or was never introduced in Southeast Asia and Tibet, though they slowly started being reintroduced by nuns such as {{ill|Ayya Khema|en|Ayya Khema|qid=Q89274|lt=Ayya Khema|1=Ayya Khema}}, {{ill|Dhammananda Bhikkhuni|en|Dhammananda Bhikkhuni|qid=Q538931|lt=Dhammananda Bhikkhuni|1=Dhammananda Bhikkhuni}}, {{ill|Tenzin Palmo|en|Tenzin Palmo|qid=Q2544516|lt=Tenzin Palmo|1=Tenzin Palmo}} and {{ill|Thubten Chodron|en|Thubten Chodron|qid=Q7798660|lt=Thubten Chodron|1=Thubten Chodron}}. Until very recently, China, Taiwan and Korea were the only places where fully ordained bhiksuni lineages still existed. An international conference of Buddhist nuns was held in February 1987 at {{ill|Bodh Gaya|en|Bodh Gaya|qid=Q176767|lt=Bodh Gaya|1=Bodh Gaya}} and saw the formation of 'Sakyadhita' (Daughters of the Buddha) the International Association of Buddhist Women which focuses on helping Buddhist nuns throughout the world.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 400.</ref> === Relationships === The Buddha placed much importance on the cultivation of good will and compassion towards one's parents, spouse, friends and all other beings. Buddhism strongly values harmony in the family and community. Keeping the five precepts and having a generous attitude (Dana) is seen as the foundation for this harmony. An important text, seen as the lay people's Vinaya (code of conduct) is the [[:en:Sigalovada Sutta|Sigalovada Sutta]] which outlines wrong action and warns against the squandering of wealth. The [[:en:Sigalovada Sutta|Sigalovada Sutta]] outlines how a virtuous person "worships the six directions" which are parents (East), teachers (South), wife (West), and friends and colleagues (North), and the two vertical directions as: ascetics and Brahmins (Up) and the Servants (Down). The text elaborates on how to respect and support them, and how in turn the Six will return the kindness and support. The relationships are based on reciprocation, and it is understood one has no right to expect behavior from others unless one also performs good acts in his favor. Parents for example, are to be respected and supported with the understanding that they are to have provided care and affection to oneself. In marriage, the sutta states that a householder should treat his wife by "being courteous to her, by not despising her, by being faithful to her, by handing over authority to her, by providing her with adornments." while in return the wife "performs her duties well, she is hospitable to relations and attendants, she is faithful, she protects what he brings, she is skilled and industrious in discharging her duties."<ref>Narada Thera (trans), Sigalovada Sutta: The Discourse to Sigala The Layperson's Code of Discipline, {{cite web |url=http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/dn/dn.31.0.nara.html |title=Sigalovada Sutta: The Discourse to Sigala |access-date=2012-06-06 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160518095524/http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/dn/dn.31.0.nara.html |archive-date=2016-05-18 |qq=}}</ref> The Buddha also stated that a wife and husband are to be each other's best friend (parama sakha). While monogamy is the predominant model for marriage, Buddhist societies have also practiced and accepted {{ill|Polygamy|en|Polygamy|qid=Q170585|lt=Polygamy|1=Polygamy}} and {{ill|polyandry|en|polyandry|qid=Q2102979|lt=polyandry|1=Polyandry}}.<ref>Peter Harvey An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics Cambridge University Press 2000, page 101.</ref> Buddhism sees marriage not as sacred but as a secular partnership and hence has no issue with divorce. === Sexuality{{anchor|Sexual misconduct|qq=}} === {{Main|Buddhism and sexuality|qq=}} The Third (or sometimes Fourth) of the [[:en:Five Precepts|Five Precepts]] of Buddhism states that one is to refrain from "{{ill|sexual misconduct|en|sexual misconduct|qid=Q7458798|lt=sexual misconduct|1=Sexual misconduct}}", which has various interpretations, but generally entails any sexual conduct which is harmful to others, such as rape, molestation and often adultery, although this depends on the local marriage and relationship customs. Buddhist {{ill|Monk|en|Monk|qid=Q733786|lt=Monk|1=Monk}}s and {{ill|Nun|en|Nun|qid=Q191808|lt=Nun|1=Nun}}s of most traditions are not only expected to refrain from all sexual activity but also take [[:en:vows of celibacy|vows of celibacy]]. ==== Sexual orientation ==== {{Main|Buddhism and sexual orientation|qq=}} Among the Buddhist traditions there is a vast diversity of opinion about {{ill|homosexuality|en|homosexuality|qid=Q6636|lt=homosexuality|1=Homosexuality}}, and in interpreting the precedents which define "sexual misconduct" generally. Though there is no explicit condemnation of homosexuality in Buddhist sutras, be it {{ill|Theravada|en|Theravada|qid=Q132265|lt=Theravada|1=Theravada}}, {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} or [[:en:Mantrayana|Mantrayana]], societal and community attitudes and the historical view of practitioners have established precedents. Some ''[[:en:Sangha (Buddhism)|sangha]]'' equate homosexuality with scriptural sexual misconduct prohibited by the [[:en:Five Precepts|Five Precepts]]. Other ''sangha'' hold that if sexuality is compassionate and/or consensual and does not contravene vows, then there is no karmic infraction, irrespective of whether it is same-sex or not. Buddhist communities in Western states as well as in Japan generally tend to be accepting of homosexuality. In Japan, homosexual relations among Buddhist samurai and clergy were actually quite common. Male homosexuality between clergy was especially common in the Tantric {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon school|1=Shingon Buddhism}}.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 427.</ref> According to the [[:en:Pāli Canon|Pāli Canon]] and {{ill|Āgama (Buddhism)|en|Āgama (Buddhism)|qid=Q1153367|lt=Āgama|1=Āgama (Buddhism)}} (the Early Buddhist scriptures), there is no saying that same or opposite gender relations have anything to do with sexual misconduct,<ref name="cunda kammaraputta sutta">{{cite web|title=Cunda Kammaraputta Sutta|trans-title=To Cunda the Silversmith|others=Translated from the Pali by Thanissaro Bhikkhu|publisher=Access to Insight|year=1997|id=[[:en:Anguttara Nikaya|AN]] 10.176|url=http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/an/an10/an10.176.than.html| access-date = 2011-03-14|quote=Abandoning sensual misconduct, he abstains from sensual misconduct. He does not get sexually involved with those who are protected by their mothers, their fathers, their brothers, their sisters, their relatives, or their Dhamma; those with husbands, those who entail punishments, or even those crowned with flowers by another man|qq=}}</ref><ref>[http://www.dhammatalks.net/suttacentral/sc/zh/ma128.html 優婆塞經] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170218064551/http://www.dhammatalks.net/suttacentral/sc/zh/ma128.html |date=2017-02-18 |qq=}}(Upāsaka Sutra from Madhyam āgama):復次，舍梨子！白衣聖弟子離邪婬、斷邪婬，彼或有父所護，或母所護，或父母所護，或兄弟所護，或姉妹所護，或婦父母所護，或親親所護，或同姓所護，或為他婦女，有鞭罰恐怖，及有名雇債至華鬘親，不犯如是女。彼於邪淫淨除其心，白衣聖弟子善護行，此第三法</ref> and some Theravada monks express that same-gender relations do not violate the rule to avoid sexual misconduct, which means not having sex with someone underage (thus protected by their parents or guardians), someone betrothed or married and who have taken vows of religious celibacy.<ref>* {{cite web|author1=Ajahn Punnadhammo|title=Same Sex Marriage|url=http://www.arrowriver.ca/torStar/samesex.html|quote=The lay man is told to abstain from sex with "unsuitable partners" defined as girls under age, women betrothed or married and women who have taken vows of religious celibacy. This is clear, sound advice and seems to suggest that sexual misconduct is that which would disrupt existing family or love relationships. This is consonant with the general Buddhist principle that that which causes suffering for oneself or others is unethical behaviour. ("Unskillful behaviour" would be closer to the original.) There is no good reason to assume that '''''homosexual relations which do not violate this principle''''' should be treated differently. |qq=}} * {{cite book|author1=Somdet Phra Buddhaghosacariya|title=Uposatha Sila The Eight-Precept Observance|date=1993|url=http://www.accesstoinsight.org/lib/authors/nanavara/uposatha.html|qq=}}There are '''four factors of the third precept''' (kamesu micchacara) #agamaniya vatthu — that which should not be visited (the 20 groups of women). #asmim sevana-cittam — the intention to have intercourse with anyone included in the above-mentioned groups. #sevanap-payogo — the effort at sexual intercourse. #maggena maggappatipatti — sexual contact through that adhivasanam effort. * {{cite book|author1=Bhikkhu Bodhi|title=Going for Refuge & Taking the Precepts (The Five Precepts)|date=1981|publisher=Buddhist Publication Society|url=http://bodhimonastery.org/going-for-refuge-taking-the-precepts.html#prec2|qq=}}</ref> Some later traditions, like {{ill|Shantideva|en|Shantideva|qid=Q445460|lt=Shantideva|1=Shantideva}} and {{ill|Gampopa|en|Gampopa|qid=Q453191|lt=Gampopa|1=Gampopa}}, feature restrictions on non-vaginal sex (including homosexuality). A medieval commentary of the [[:en:Digha Nikaya|Digha Nikaya]] mentions examples of immorality in society, and one of the examples is homosexuality, whereas this has no basis in the Sutta.<ref>{{cite web|last=Sujato|first=Ajahn|author-link=Ajahn Sujato|title=Why Buddhists Should Support Marriage Equality|url=https://sujato.wordpress.com/2012/03/21/1430/|date=2012-03-20|qq=}}</ref> Other Buddhist texts such as the [[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharma-kosa]] and the {{ill|Jataka tales|en|Jataka tales|qid=Q668439|lt=Jataka tales|1=Jataka tales}} make no mention of homosexuality in this regard. According to Jose Ignacio Cabezon, Buddhist cultures' attitudes towards homosexuality have generally been neutral.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 423.</ref> While both men and women can be ordained, [[:en:hermaphrodites|hermaphrodites]] are not allowed by the {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|qid=Q848764|lt=Vinaya|1=Vinaya}}. According to the ancient texts this is because of the possibility that they will seduce monks or nuns.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 413.</ref> The Vinaya also prevents ''[[Buddhism and sexual orientation#Paṇḍakas|pandakas]]'' from becoming monastics, which have been defined as "without testicles" and generally referred to those who lacked the normal (usually physical) characteristics of maleness (in some cases it refers to women who lack the normal characteristics of femaleness). This rule was established by the Buddha after a pandaka monk broke the Vinaya precepts by having relations with others. Therefore, it seems that pandakas were initially allowed into the Sangha. Later Buddhist texts like the {{ill|Milinda Panha|en|Milinda Panha|qid=Q497089|lt=Milinda Panha|1=Milinda Panha}} and the [[:en:Abhidharma-kosa|Abhidharma-kosa]] see pandakas as being spiritually hindered by their sexuality and mental defilements. === Economic ethics === [[File:Bhutan Gross National Happiness.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Bhutan|en|Bhutan|qid=Q917|lt=Bhutan|1=Bhutan}}'s government promotes the concept of '{{ill|Gross National Happiness|en|Gross National Happiness|qid=Q862253|lt=Gross National Happiness|1=Gross National Happiness}}' (GNH), based on Buddhist spiritual values.]] {{Main|Buddhist economics|qq=}} Buddha's teachings to laypeople included advice on how to make their living and how to use their wealth. The Buddha considered the creation of wealth to be praiseworthy, so long as it was done morally,<ref name="Peter Harvey 2000, page 187">Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 187 {{ISBN|0521556406|qq=}}</ref> in accordance with right livelihood, one of the elements of the Noble Eightfold Path, and which refers to making one's living without killing, being complicit in the suffering of other beings (by selling weapons, poison, alcohol or flesh) or through lying, stealing or deceit.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 188 {{ISBN|0521556406|qq=}}</ref> The Sigalovada Sutta states that a master should look after servants and employees by: "(1) by assigning them work according to their ability, (2) by supplying them with food and with wages, (3) by tending them in sickness, (4) by sharing with them any delicacies, (5) by granting them leave at times" ([[:en:Digha Nikaya|Digha Nikaya]] 31). Early Buddhist texts see success in work as aided by one's spiritual and moral qualities. In the Adiya Sutta the Buddha also outlined several ways in which people could put their 'righteously gained' wealth to use:<ref>AN 5.41, Adiya Sutta: Benefits to be Obtained (from Wealth) translated from the Pali by Thanissaro Bhikkhu, {{cite web|url=http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/an/an05/an05.041.than.html |title=Adiya Sutta: Benefits to be Obtained (from Wealth) |access-date=2015-12-02 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160420202447/http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/an/an05/an05.041.than.html |archive-date=2016-04-20 |qq=}}</ref> #Providing 'pleasure & satisfaction' to themselves, their mother & father, their children, spouse, slaves, servants, & assistants. #Providing 'pleasure & satisfaction' to their friends and associates. #Warding off calamities coming from fire, flood, kings, thieves, or hateful heirs, and keeps himself safe. #Performs five oblations/offerings: to relatives, guests, the dead, kings, & devas. #Giving of offerings to priests ([[:en:Brahmins|Brahmins]]) and contemplatives (monks). The Buddha placed much emphasis on the virtue of giving and sharing, and hence the practice of donating and charity are central to Buddhist economic ethics. Even the poor are encouraged to share, because this brings about greater spiritual wealth: "If beings knew, as I know, the results of giving & sharing, they would not eat without having given, nor would the stain of selfishness overcome their minds. Even if it were their last bite, their last mouthful, they would not eat without having shared, if there were someone to receive their gift."<ref>Itivuttaka: The Group of Ones translated from the Pali by Thanissaro Bhikkhu, {{cite web |url=http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/kn/iti/iti.1.001-027.than.html |title=Itivuttaka: The Group of Ones |access-date=2015-12-02 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160518095639/http://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/kn/iti/iti.1.001-027.than.html |archive-date=2016-05-18 |qq=}}</ref> The modern growth of {{ill|Engaged Buddhism|en|Engaged Buddhism|qid=Q472716|lt=Engaged Buddhism|1=Engaged Buddhism}} has seen an emphasis on social work and charity. Buddhist aid and activist organizations include {{ill|Buddhist Global Relief|en|Buddhist Global Relief|qid=Q28368924|lt=Buddhist Global Relief|1=Buddhist Global Relief}}, Lotus Outreach, {{ill|Buddhist Peace Fellowship|en|Buddhist Peace Fellowship|qid=Q2867353|lt=Buddhist Peace Fellowship|1=Buddhist Peace Fellowship}}, Piyarra Kutta, {{ill|International Network of Engaged Buddhists|en|International Network of Engaged Buddhists|qid=Q1667794|lt=International Network of Engaged Buddhists|1=International Network of Engaged Buddhists}}, The {{ill|Tzu Chi|en|Tzu Chi|qid=Q717899|lt=Tzu Chi|1=Tzu Chi}} Foundation, Nonviolent Peaceforce, and {{ill|Zen Peacemakers|en|Zen Peacemakers|qid=Q8068963|lt=Zen Peacemakers|1=Zen Peacemakers}}. Buddhist texts promote the building of public works which benefit the community and stories of Buddhist Kings like {{ill|Ashoka|en|Ashoka|qid=Q8589|lt=Ashoka|1=Ashoka}} are used as an example of lay people who promoted the public welfare by building hospitals and parks for the people. The Buddha's chief lay disciple, the rich merchant {{ill|Anathapindika|en|Anathapindika|qid=Q2264464|lt=Anathapindika|1=Anathapindika}} ('Feeder of the Poor') is also another example of a virtuous layperson who donated much of his wealth for the benefit of others and was thus known as the "foremost disciple in generosity". Early Buddhist texts do not disparage merchants and trade, but instead promote enterprise as long as it is done ethically and leads to the well-being of the community. The gold standard for rulers in Buddhism is the ideal wheel turning king, the [[:en:Chakravartin|Chakravartin]]. A Chakravartin is said to rule justly, giving to the needy and combating poverty so as to prevent social unrest. A Chakravartin does not fight wars for gain but only in defense of the kingdom, he accepts immigrants and refugees, and builds hospitals, parks, hostels, wells, canals and rest houses for the people and animals.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 198.</ref> {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} maintains that lay Bodhisattvas should engage in social welfare activities for the good and safety of others.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 191.</ref> In the lands of Southern Buddhism, Buddhist monasteries often became places were the poor, destitute, orphaned, elderly can take shelter. Monasteries often provided education and took care of the sick, and therefore are also centers of social welfare for the poor. {{ill|Robert Thurman|en|Robert Thurman|qid=Q1377791|lt=Robert Thurman|1=Robert Thurman}}, in his discussion of {{ill|Nagarjuna|en|Nagarjuna|qid=Q171195|lt=Nagarjuna|1=Nagarjuna}}'s ''Precious Garland Ratnavali'' sees the Mahayana Buddhist tradition as politically supporting "a {{ill|welfare state|en|welfare state|qid=Q192451|lt=welfare state|1=Welfare state}} ...a rule of compassionate {{ill|Socialism|en|Socialism|qid=Q7272|lt=Socialism|1=Socialism}}".<ref>Thurman, Robert. Social and Cultural rights in Buddhism, {{cite web|url=http://enlight.lib.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-MISC/misc30574.pdf |title=Archived copy |access-date=2015-12-02 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161020222155/http://enlight.lib.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-MISC/misc30574.pdf |archive-date=2016-10-20 |qq=}}</ref> Prominent {{ill|Buddhist socialism|en|Buddhist socialism|qid=Q2632571|lt=Buddhist socialists|1=Buddhist socialism}} include the [[14th Dalai Lama#Economics and political stance|14th Dalai Lama]], [[:en:Buddhadasa|Buddhadasa]], {{ill|B. R. Ambedkar|en|B. R. Ambedkar|qid=Q231690|lt=B. R. Ambedkar|1=B. R. Ambedkar}}, {{ill|U Nu|en|U Nu|qid=Q316384|lt=U Nu|1=U Nu}}, [[:en:Girō Seno'o|Girō Seno'o]] and Lin Qiuwu.<ref>Charles B. Jones, Buddhism and Marxism in Taiwan: Lin Qiuwu's Religious Socialism and Its Legacy in Modern Times, {{cite web |url=http://www.globalbuddhism.org/1/jones001.html |title=Buddhism and Marxism in Taiwan: Lin Qiuwu's Religious Socialism and Its Legacy in Modern Times |access-date=2015-12-02 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304200527/http://www.globalbuddhism.org/1/jones001.html |archive-date=2016-03-04 |qq=}}</ref> Others such as {{ill|Neville Karunatilake|en|Neville Karunatilake|qid=Q7004750|lt=Neville Karunatilake|1=Neville Karunatilake}}, {{ill|E. F. Schumacher|en|E. F. Schumacher|qid=Q76526|lt=E. F. Schumacher|1=E. F. Schumacher}}, Padmasiri De Silva, [[:en:Prayudh Payutto|Prayudh Payutto]] and {{ill|Sulak Sivaraksa|en|Sulak Sivaraksa|qid=Q1356452|lt=Sulak Sivaraksa|1=Sulak Sivaraksa}} have promoted a {{ill|Buddhist economics|en|Buddhist economics|qid=Q851366|lt=Buddhist economics|1=Buddhist economics}} that does not necessarily define itself as socialist but still offers a critique of modern {{ill|consumer capitalism|en|consumer capitalism|qid=Q5164689|lt=consumer capitalism|1=Consumer capitalism}}. {{ill|E. F. Schumacher|en|E. F. Schumacher|qid=Q76526|lt=E. F. Schumacher|1=E. F. Schumacher}} in his "Buddhist economics" (1973) wrote: "Buddhist economics must be very different from the economics of modern materialism, since the Buddhist sees the essence of civilisation not in a multiplication of human wants but in the purification of human character." While modern economics seeks to satisfy human desires, Buddhism seeks to reduce our desires and hence Buddhist economics would tend to promote a sense of {{ill|anti-consumerism|en|anti-consumerism|qid=Q1783091|lt=anti-consumerism|1=Anti-consumerism}} and {{ill|Simple living|en|Simple living|qid=Q56092|lt=Simple living|1=Simple living}}. In his ''Buddhist Economics: A Middle Way for the Market Place'', [[:en:Prayudh Payutto|Prayudh Payutto]] writes that consumption is only a means to an end which is 'development of human potential' and 'well being within the individual, within society and within the environment'. From a Buddhist perspective then, 'Right consumption' is based on {{ill|well-being|en|well-being|qid=Q7981051|lt=well-being|1=Well-being}} while 'wrong consumption' is the need to 'satisfy the desire for pleasing sensations or ego-gratification'.<ref>Payutto, Buddhist Economics A Middle Way for the Market Place, chapter three. {{cite web|url=http://www.urbandharma.org/udharma2/becono3.html |title=Buddhist Economics... Part 3 |access-date=2015-12-02 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161024024539/http://www.urbandharma.org/udharma2/becono3.html |archive-date=2016-10-24 |qq=}}</ref> Similarly, {{ill|Sulak Sivaraksa|en|Sulak Sivaraksa|qid=Q1356452|lt=Sulak Sivaraksa|1=Sulak Sivaraksa}} argues that "the religion of {{ill|consumerism|en|consumerism|qid=Q89640|lt=consumerism|1=Consumerism}} emphasizes greed, hatred and delusion" which causes anxiety and that this must be countered with an ethic of satisfaction<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 220.</ref> Modern attempts to practice Buddhist economics can be seen in the {{ill|Sarvodaya Shramadana Movement|en|Sarvodaya Shramadana Movement|qid=Q2253980|lt=Sarvodaya Shramadana Movement|1=Sarvodaya Shramadana Movement}} and in the {{ill|Gross National Happiness|en|Gross National Happiness|qid=Q862253|lt=Gross National Happiness|1=Gross National Happiness}} economics of Bhutan. While Buddhism encourages wealth gained ethically,<ref name="Peter Harvey 2000, page 187"/> it sees greed and craving for riches as negative, and praises contentment as 'the greatest wealth'. {{ill|Poverty|en|Poverty|qid=Q10294|lt=Poverty|1=Poverty}} and debt are seen as causes of suffering, immorality, and social unrest if they prevent one from having basic necessities and peace of mind. For laypeople, Buddhism promotes the middle way between a life of poverty and a materialistic or consumerist life in which one is always seeking to enrich oneself and to buy more things.<ref>Peter Harvey ''An Introduction to Buddhist Ethics'' Cambridge University Press 2000, page 195.</ref> For Buddhist laypersons then, to be Buddhist does not mean to reject all material things, but, according to Sizemore and Swearer: "it specifies an attitude to be cultivated and expressed in whatever material condition one finds oneself. To be non-attached is to possess and use material things but not to be possessed or used by them. Therefore, the idea of non-attachment applies all across Buddhist society, to laymen and monk alike."<ref>Russell F. Sizemore and Donald K. Swearer, "Introduction" to Sizemore and Swearer, eds., Ethics, Wealth and Salvation: A Study in Buddhist Social Ethics (Columbia, South Carolina: University of South Carolina, 1990), p. 2.</ref> ==
